basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Sharḥ — “The Opening”, “The Dilation” — is an early Meccan sūra, sister to al-Ḍuḥā (sūra 93). Like her, it is addressed to the Prophet at a moment when he carries heavy burdens; like her, it comes as a balm. The word sharḥ (شَرْح) comes from the verb sharaḥa (شَرَحَ), “to open, to widen, to dilate” — as one opens a tightened thing. The sūra opens with three rhetorical questions that are so many recallings of grace: have We not dilated your breast? Lifted off your burden? Raised your renown? Qushayrī, reading after reading, unfolds: “Have We not widened your heart for islām? Have We not softened it for faith? Have We not widened your breast for it to receive what We send down upon you?” The burden lifted, he says, is the very weight of the prophetic mission: God made the Prophet a borne one, not a bearer (maḥmūl, lā mutaḥammil, مَحْمُول لَا مُتَحَمِّل) — and this, for the commentator, is the highest degree of love. Then comes the promise: with hardship there is ease, doubled so that the heart will learn it. And the sūra closes on the essential command: toward your Lord, turn with longing.

versets.trad: Have We not opened your breast for you?
versets.commentaire: First question, first grace. nashraḥ ṣadrak (نَشْرَحْ صَدْرَك): We have widened your breast, dilated your heart. Qushayrī unfolds the verb in several depths: “Have We not widened your heart for islām? Have We not softened it for faith? Have We not dilated your breast with the light of the messengership? Have We not widened it for it to receive what We send down upon you?” Four successive enlargements, like four concentric circles of one same gift. The dilated breast is the heart made capable. Capable of islām (handing oneself over to God), of faith (trustful adherence), of the heavy mission of prophet, and of the Word itself. The Quran descends only upon a heart that has first been made large.

mots.def: interrogative particle + negation particle for the apocopated imperfect.
sharaḥa: to open, to widen, to dilate, to unfold; apocopated imperfect 1st pers. plur.
preposition li- + 2nd pers. masc. sing. suffix pronoun.
ṣadr: the breast, seat of the inmost heart; with suffix pronoun.

versets.commentaire: waḍaʿnā (وَضَعْنَا): We set down, deposited, took off. wizr (وِزْر): the burden, sometimes also the weight of sin. Qushayrī offers three delicate readings. The first: the sin from before prophethood (the Prophet, kept from sin, had only youthful hesitations). The second: “We preserved you from falling into sin, and therefore lifted it from you since you never deserved it.” The third, the most beautiful: “We lightened upon you the weights of prophethood, and made you carried, not carrier.” To become maḥmūl — borne by God — while other prophets were mutaḥammil — those who bear by their own strength: this, Qushayrī says, is “the highest degree of love”. The beloved servant no longer has to carry — it is God who carries him.

mots.def: waḍaʿa: to set down, to lay aside, to lift off; perfect 1st pers. plur.
wizr: burden, load; sometimes also: weight of sin. With suffix pronoun.

versets.trad: the burden that weighed upon your back?
versets.commentaire: anqaḍa ẓahrak (أَنقَضَ ظَهْرَك): “it cracked your back”, “it made it groan”. The verb has a concrete violence — the sound of too heavy a load on a spine. Qushayrī: “Without Our lightening, it would have broken you.” The verse acknowledges the gravity of what the load would have been had it not been lifted. For the Sufi, the verse also resounds in every life: there are, in every life, burdens the servant does not know have been lightened. Right gratitude is to thank not only for what has been received, but for what one has not had to carry.

mots.def: anqaḍa: to make crack, to tear, to break under weight; perfect.

versets.trad: And have We not raised high your renown?
versets.commentaire: rafaʿnā dhikrak (رَفَعْنَا ذِكْرَك): We have raised your name, exalted your mention. Qushayrī puts in God's mouth a striking word: “We have raised your mention by Our own mention. For just as the formula of the shahāda is not valid without Me, it is not valid without you either.” An allusion to the double formula: lā ilāha illā Llāh, Muḥammadun rasūlu Llāh — there is no god but God, Muḥammad is the Messenger of God. The Prophet's name has been bound to God's Name in the very prayer that opens Islam: where God is named, the Prophet too is named. It is the extreme image of an elevation through divine companionship. Or, Qushayrī says further: “We have raised your mention by the people's saying: Muḥammad is the Messenger of God.”

mots.def: rafaʿa: to raise, to exalt; perfect 1st pers. plur.
dhikr: mention, remembrance, renown; with suffix pronoun.

versets.trad: Truly with hardship there is ease!
versets.commentaire: A pivotal verse, which will be doubled. al-ʿusr (الْعُسْر): hardship; yusr (يُسْر): ease, relief. The preposition is essential: maʿa — not “after”, but with. The ease is not a future to wait for; it is present within the trial itself, beside it, simultaneous. Qushayrī recalls the ḥadīth that lights up the grammar of the verse: “A single hardship will not overcome two eases.” And he explains: hardship is named with the article (al-) at both occurrences — so it is one and the same hardship that returns. But ease is indefinite at both occurrences — so they are two distinct eases. One in this life (a relief, a way out), the other in the next (the reward). One hardship, two ways out: the promise is mathematically generous.

mots.def: ʿusr: hardship, difficulty; with the definite article.

versets.trad: Truly with hardship there is ease!
versets.commentaire: The doubling is not a minor stylistic repetition: it grounds, as we saw, Qushayrī's reading. One hardship, two eases. The pedagogy is also practical: God repeats the sentence so that the heart will learn it, so that it will lodge in the soul and rise up of itself in low hours. There is, in the simple fact of saying again, a caress. It is the opposite of the sermon: it is consolation leaning twice over the same heart, to make sure it has been heard.

versets.trad: So when you are free from one task, labour at the next,
versets.commentaire: The key verse for the spiritual life. faraghta (فَرَغْتَ): you have emptied yourself, you have finished. fa-nṣab (فَانصَبْ): rise up to effort, labour. Qushayrī unfolds the verse in three readings that complete one another admirably. The first: “When you have finished the prescribed prayer, labour in supplication.” One work ended calls another; there is no threshold of rest for the servant. The second: “When you have finished worshipping, labour in intercession.” Once you have given God His due, give your brothers what you can. The third, the most inward: “When you have finished worshipping with your soul, labour with your heart.” Outer work done, inner work begins; and it does not end. The Sufi hears here the whole grammar of the spiritual life: one does not rest before the encounter.

mots.def: faragha: to be empty, to be free, to have finished; perfect 2nd pers. masc. sing.
naṣaba: to stand up, to labour, to exert oneself; imperative.

versets.trad: and toward your Lord turn with longing.
versets.commentaire: The last word of the last verse is fa-rghab (فَارْغَب) — long for ardently, yearn, turn with desire. The root r-gh-b evokes intense desire; here all positive: it is raghba (رَغْبَة), the spiritual avidity for God. Qushayrī sums up: “Toward your Lord, long — in every state.” Not only in the night of prayer, not only in trial, not only in joy. In every state. This is the final posture of the believer, and the whole point of the sūra: the breast has been dilated, the burden lifted, the name raised, ease promised, effort kept up — all of this leads to one single, simple movement, of which every breath of a life can be the occasion: to turn toward God with longing.

mots.def: raghiba: to long for, to yearn ardently; imperative.
